have it pretty well figured out already.
use a mix of a tool called recraft with a workflow and it works pretty well.

What we have now, I'll show you real quick, we have this little app that we build just to get next to one of our workflows, so you can input whatever image you want, and you can input any text that you want, and then you get multiple versions of the graphic, and it will generate on the fly, and this is just like one of the workflows, but the idea here is to take the whole article, be able to find which are the things that should be illustrated, and trigger a bunch of calls like this, so you would end up with like having a bunch of different versions or things that you can use, like in this case, we generated this image, and we passed the text from that one of this, I just grabbed something from here, you could just grab anything, essentially, you could just like grab this, and put it there, and it will trigger a workflow that will figure out how to illustrate this, and given the case,
Context of that illustration.

So what is happening here, we're using like a vision, vision capability from Claude to like read the image, analyze the composition of the image, analyze the background, analyze the color, analyze the contrast, and then it will rewrite the parts of the text and then figure out where to put it and generate as many variations as you want.
So here we have five variations, can have whatever size you want, and you ended up with an image at the end that will save.
So this is just like to show how it would, just to play with it, but at the end of the day, would be integrated the same way as we do with all our other workflows.

I don't think I showed it last time during our all-hands.
But essentially, this is something that Kirkland built, and I just built a little app on top, and the idea here is that we, like, improved our fact checker quite a bit.
Like, we used to have a fact checker that ran in air ops with a bunch of different things, but it was pretty limited, so you couldn't, like, ask a bunch of questions.
What we want to do is, like, when you have an article, you want to, like, parse out the article, figure out all the statements and all the things that should be fact-checked.
So that goes from, like, statements, just code snippets, to data, to anything that a person would look at and say, like, this should probably be fact-checked.
So that's...
What we do, that's what we've always done, but we were limited by the amount of things we could run at once with Aero Ops.
We moved it out of Aero Ops and moved to our infrastructure, so now we can do as many questions as we want.
So in this case, for example, I this fake article here that starts with everything is essentially wrong.
And in this case, what we want to do is just extract all the things that could be wrong and revalidate them using a good search engine like perplexity.

So in that, I just fed that URL to the workflow and we got back the detailed analysis that is essentially showing all the things that are wrong and the suggested correction and the reason why it thinks this should be wrong and the data that we found when it was validating the questions.
So what's happening behind the scenes here, if you look into our...
Studio is this.

go in, we get the, we get the, I was just using the abnormal, abnormal, it's another one that I was testing here, so abnormal, we've got everything right, so like 100% accurate, and we have an accuracy score, that would just look for things that are, it's not going to do anything if it's not for sure wrong, but it would just flag it.

So, if we had a person in a loop here, we could just double check those things, usually the thing that's not fully sure are things that are marketing statements, or product announcements, things that are, they're not really like available on the internet to get data from, but if we had documents that could be used to be flag checked, we could also input that here, but in this case, in the case of abnormal, what it did, it's just like fetch the article, so we received an URL, we parse the URL, we parse the article back, and then we split it into chunks, in this case, I think,
It's just one big chunk.
It's not large enough for multiple chunks.
And we look at every passage and we look for things that could be fact-checked.
And we put here a reason why it thinks it should be fact-checked and come up with a bunch of questions.

And then we will, like, execute each one of these questions against perplexity and validate if, based on the data that we got back from perplexity, if it thinks it's correct, and assign a confidence score in the Likert scale from, like, minus two to two, and then suggest a rewrite.
And the final version, we just, like, go through everything and rewrite everything that's wrong.
We don't rewrite anything that's not sure.
So we expect that this would be, like, a lot better than what we were doing before.

But the fact checker may return unsure if there's simply no information on the web, which may happen a lot with early stage companies, you know, that are just launching their product.

And so when it comes down to it, the feedback.

Okay, great, thank you, and then we'll integrate that into our writing.

So a little bit of human touch there until the fact checker gets better or more educated on that company.

So if you're doing something for, like, Strappy, and they are doing a blog post about Next.js, what happens if we point the fact checker just to the Next.js documentation, if we will be able to fix all the code snippets?
I'm not sure.

So for Strappy, we have a version of the fact checker using kappa.ai, which is something that turns the documentation websites of technical tools into a Q&A chat.
So we use that instead of perplexity, and we'll see how that goes.
But we're also trying to figure out if there is a way for us to execute the codes.
if there are codes snippets, if we can execute the code and see if that works.
So like we're thinking about all these technical clients we have, fact-checking codes snippets is like a big deal.
